title: How you point at one particular thing
testCase: Do the listings have a class, while the last paragraph does not?
blocks.0.text: Four paragraphs. Three of them are bike listings. The fourth is not — it is a note.
blocks.1.text: It all works. Nothing is broken. That is why this lesson is harder than the last ones — the problem does not show until you want to do something.
blocks.3.text: You want the listings in a box with a grey background. The note below — no, it stays plain text.
blocks.4.text:
Now say it. Out loud, to the machine. "Make… what?" All four are

. To the browser they are the same thing, four times. There is no way to say "these three, but not the last one", because you have nothing to tell them apart by.
blocks.5.text: You cannot change what you cannot point at.
blocks.7.text: There are two attributes for exactly this. They go on any tag and do nothing visible — they simply give you something to grab it by.
blocks.9.text: There. Now the three listings share a name and the note does not. You CAN now say "everything named listing goes in a grey box" — and the note will not be touched.
blocks.11.text: Above is the result you will get once you reach CSS. Today you only hand out the names. But without them, the next step is impossible.
blocks.13.items.0: class — the name of a GROUP. Many things can carry it. "These are of one kind."
blocks.13.items.1: id — the name of ONE thing. Only one per page. "This is precisely it."
blocks.15.text: One thing can carry both. The red bike is a listing (like the other two) AND it is the best deal (the only one). The two names say different things.
blocks.17.items.0: id is unique. Two identical ids on one page is an error — like two people sharing one ID number.
blocks.17.items.1: One thing can have many classes, separated by spaces: class="listing sold".
blocks.17.items.2: No spaces inside a name. Write best-deal, not "best deal" — otherwise it becomes two classes.
blocks.17.items.3: Latin letters, lowercase. Capitals work, but nobody writes them.
blocks.19.text: Here is the most common mistake, and it is the same one you have seen twice already: class="grey-box". It works. And it is a trap — tomorrow you decide the box should be blue, but the class is called "grey". Either you rename it everywhere, or you live with a lie in your code.
blocks.20.text: class="listing" never goes stale. It says what the thing IS, not how it looks today.
blocks.21.text: Your turn. Give the three listings a shared name, and the note none. And put an id on the best deal.
